entities.name: CHAPTER 2: CREATING A MULTIVERSE
entities.canonicalText: The text is narrative exposition about the multiverse and planar adventures, not a discrete game entity. It describes setting and concepts rather than defining specific rules, spells, monsters, or feats.

entities.name: The Planes
entities.canonicalText: The text is a narrative explanation of planar concepts rather than a discrete game entity with structured attributes.

entities.name: Planar Categories
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading and narrative description of planar categories, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Putting the Planes Together
entities.canonicalText: This is narrative guidance on cosmology and campaign setup, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Inventing Your Own Planes
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading providing design guidelines for creating custom planes, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Spells
entities.canonicalText: The text contains narrative examples and explanations about spells, but does not present discrete game entities like specific spells in a structured rule format. It describes spell mechanics through storytelling rather than official rulebook formatting.

entries.name: Astral Plane
entries.description: The realm of thought and dream, where visitors travel as disembodied souls to reach the Outer Planes. It is a great silvery sea, the same above and below, with swirling wisps of white and gray streaking among motes of light like distant stars. Most of the Astral Sea is a vast, empty expanse. Visitors occasionally stumble upon the petrified corpse of a dead god or other chunks of rock drifting forever in the silvery void. Much more commonplace are color pools—magical pools of colored light that flicker like radiant, spinning coins.
features: Creatures on the Astral Plane don't age or suffer from hunger or thirst.
A traveler in the Astral Plane can move by simply thinking about moving, but distance has little meaning.
In combat, a creature's walking speed (in feet) is equal to 3 × its Intelligence score. The smarter a creature is, the easier it can control its movement by act of will.

entries: Traveling through the Astral Plane by means of the astral projection spell involves projecting one's consciousness there, usually in search of a gateway to an Outer Plane to visit. Since the Outer Planes are as much spiritual states of being as they are physical places, this allows a character to manifest in an Outer Plane as if he or she had physically traveled there, but as in a dream. A character's death—either in the Astral Plane or on the destination plane—causes no actual harm. Only the severing of a character's silver cord while on the Astral Plane (or the death of his or her helpless physical body on the Material Plane) can result in the character's true death. Thus, high-level characters sometimes travel to the Outer Planes by way of astral projection rather than seek out a portal or use a more direct spell.

Only a few things can sever a traveler's silver cord, the most common being a psychic wind (described below). The legendary silver swords of the githyanki also have this ability. A character who travels bodily to the Astral Plane (by means of the plane shift spell or one of the rare portals that leads directly there) has no silver cord.

entities.name: Color Pools
entities.canonicalText: COLOR POOLS describes a planar travel mechanic rather than a discrete game entity, explaining how gateways function between planes without defining a specific entity type.

entities.name: Psychic Wind Effects
entities.canonicalText: The text describes rule effects for a d20-based mechanic but does not represent a discrete game entity (e.g., spell, feat, item). It is a rule description rather than an entity.

entities.name: Astral Plane Encounters
entities.canonicalText: The text describes narrative context about the Astral Plane's denizens and encounters, not a discrete game entity with structured rules data.

entries.name: Ethereal Plane
entries.description: A misty, fog-bound dimension. Its 'shores,' called the Border Ethereal, overlap the Material Plane and the Inner Planes. Visibility in the Border Ethereal is limited to 60 feet. The plane's depths comprise the Deep Ethereal, where visibility is limited to 30 feet.

regions.name: Border Ethereal
regions.description: Overlaps the Material Plane and Inner Planes; visibility limited to 60 feet

regions.name: Deep Ethereal
regions.description: Region of swirling mist and fog; visibility limited to 30 feet

entries.name: Border Ethereal
entries.description: A traveler can see into the overlapped plane but it appears muted and indistinct, with blurred colors and fuzzy edges. Ethereal denizens can't see beyond 30 feet into the other plane. The Ethereal Plane is usually invisible to overlapped planes without magical aid.
interactionRules: Creatures in the Border Ethereal can't attack overlapped plane creatures, and vice versa.
Ethereal travelers are invisible and silent to overlapped plane inhabitants.
Solid objects on overlapped planes don't hinder movement in the Border Ethereal, except magical effects and living beings.
specialProperties: Disobeys gravity; creatures can move up and down as easily as walking.
Ideal for reconnaissance, spying, and undetected movement.

entries.name: Deep Ethereal
entries.description: To reach the Deep Ethereal, one needs a plane shift spell or arrive by means of a gate spell or magical portal. Visitors are engulfed by roiling mist. Scattered throughout are curtains of vaporous color that connect to specific Inner Planes, the Material Plane, the Feywild, or the Shadowfell. The color of the curtain indicates the connected plane (see Ethereal Curtains table).

entities.name: Shadowfell
entities.canonicalText: The text is narrative description and does not represent a discrete game entity with structured rules data.

entities.name: Shadow Crossings
entities.canonicalText: This is a descriptive passage about a planar phenomenon, not a discrete game entity. It provides lore context rather than a structured rule or object.

entities.name: Domains of Dread
entities.canonicalText: The text describes lore and narrative about the Shadowfell and its domains, not a discrete game entity with structured rules or stats.

entities.name: Shadowfell Despair
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a game effect (Shadowfell Despair) but is presented as narrative rules text rather than a discrete entity. It contains mechanics for a condition but lacks the structured formatting of a dedicated entity entry.

entities.name: Evernight
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a narrative setting (Evernight) rather than a discrete game entity with associated rules.

entries.name: Inner Planes
entries.description: The Inner Planes surround and enfold the Material Plane and its echoes, providing the raw elemental substance from which all worlds were made. The four Elemental Planes—Air, Earth, Fire, and Water—form a ring around the Material Plane, suspended within a churning realm known as the Elemental Chaos. These planes are all connected, and the border regions between them are sometimes described as distinct planes in their own right.

entries.name: Border Regions
entries.description: At their innermost edges, where they are closest to the Material Plane (in a conceptual if not a literal geographical sense), the four Elemental Planes resemble places in the Material Plane. The four elements mingle together as they do in the Material, but the dominant element exerts a strong influence on the environment, reflecting its fundamental qualities.

entries.name: Outermost Regions
entries.description: As they extend farther from the Material Plane, the Elemental Planes become increasingly alien and hostile. Here, in the outermost regions, the elements exist in their purest form: great expanses of solid earth, blazing fire, crystal-clear water, and unsullied air. Any foreign substance is extremely rare; little air can be found in the outermost reaches of the Plane of Earth, and earth is all but impossible to find in the outermost reaches of the Plane of Fire.

entities.name: Elemental Chaos
entities.canonicalText: The text is a descriptive narrative about the Elemental Chaos as a plane of existence, not a discrete game entity with defined attributes or mechanics.

entities.name: Plane of Air
entities.canonicalText: The text describes the Plane of Air's lore and environment but does not provide structured rules data or discrete game entities like creatures, items, or mechanics.

entities.name: Plane of Earth
entities.canonicalText: The text describes the Plane of Earth's lore and geography, but contains no discrete game entity (monster, item, spell, etc.) with structured rules data.

entities.name: The City of Brass
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a narrative location but does not represent a discrete game entity with defined rules or mechanics. It is a story element rather than a structured rulebook entry.

entries.name: Plane of Water
entries.description: The nature of water is to flow... The Plane of Water is an endless sea called the Sea of Worlds... Life flourishes in the upper reaches... The deeper extents... Any land that rises above the surface... One of the few actual islands... The region of the Plane of Water nearest the Swamp of Oblivion... At the other extreme... The Frostfell... forms the border between the planes of Air and Water...

entities.name: Outer Planes
entities.canonicalText: The text is a narrative description of the Outer Planes' concept and does not represent a discrete game entity with structured rules or attributes.

entities.name: The Outer Planes
entities.canonicalText: The text describes conceptual categorization of planes (Upper/Lower) and their inhabitants, but does not represent a discrete game entity like a specific plane, creature, or rule.

entities.name: Layers of the Outer Planes
entities.canonicalText: This is a narrative description of a cosmological structure (layers of Outer Planes) rather than a discrete game entity with defined rules or attributes.

entities.name: Traveling the Outer Planes
entities.canonicalText: This is a narrative explanation of planar travel mechanics, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: The River Styx
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a location (River Styx) and its effects, but does not represent a discrete game entity with structured rules data. It contains narrative and environmental effects rather than a standalone entity with defined attributes or mechanics.

entities.name: The Infinite Staircase
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a narrative element (The Infinite Staircase) as a location, but lacks discrete game mechanics or structured data required for entity classification. It contains lore and flavor text rather than rulebook-defined entities.

entities.name: Optional Rules
entities.canonicalText: The text is a narrative explanation of optional rules, not a discrete game entity. It describes a thematic effect (psychic dissonance) but lacks the structured rule format with clear mechanics, triggers, and outcomes required for a rule entity.

entities.name: Mount Celestia
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a celestial location and includes an optional rule, but neither constitutes a discrete game entity like a creature, spell, or item. The main content is narrative worldbuilding with a rule appendix.

entries.name: Bytopia
entries.description: The Twin Paradises of Bytopia consist of a tamed, pastoral landscape and an untamed wilderness, both reflecting the plane's goodness and acceptance of law and order when necessary. It is the heaven of productive work and the satisfaction of a job well done, creating feelings of goodwill and happiness in its inhabitants.
properties: OPTIONAL RULE: PERVASIVE GOODWILL: At the end of each long rest taken on this plane, a visitor that is neither lawful good nor neutral good must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, the creature's alignment changes to lawful good or neutral good (whichever is closer to the creature's current alignment). The change becomes permanent if the creature doesn't leave the plane within 1d4 days. Otherwise, the creature's alignment reverts to normal after one day spent on a plane other than Bytopia. Casting the dispel evil and good spell on the creature also restores its original alignment.

entities.name: Elysium
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a planar location (Elysium) and its optional rule, but the structure does not match a discrete game entity like a creature, spell, or item. It contains narrative and a rule, which are not standalone entities.

entries.name: The Beastlands
entries.description: A plane of nature unbound, featuring forests ranging from moss-hung mangroves to snow-laden pines, thick jungles with no light penetration, and vast plains with vibrant wildflowers. Embodies nature's wildness and beauty, and speaks to the animal within all living things.

optionalRules.name: Hunter's Paradise
optionalRules.description: Visitors gain advantage on Wisdom (Animal Handling), Wisdom (Perception), and Wisdom (Survival) checks.

optionalRules.name: Beast Transformation
optionalRules.description: Slayers of native beasts must make a DC 10 Charisma saving throw or be transformed into the slain beast (as polymorph). The transformed creature retains intelligence and speech, can repeat the saving throw at the end of each long rest, and requires three failed saves or a remove curse spell to revert.

entities.name: Arborea
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a plane of existence (Arborea) and its associated lore/rules, but it is not a discrete game entity like a creature, object, or spell. It is a narrative description of a setting with optional mechanics.

entries.name: Ysgard
entries.description: A rugged realm of soaring mountains, deep fiords, and windswept battlefields, with summers that are long and hot, and winters that are wickedly cold and unforgiving. Its continents float above oceans of volcanic rock, below which are icy caverns so enormous as to hold entire kingdoms of giants, humans, dwarves, gnomes, and other beings. Heroes come to Ysg,ard to test their mettle not only against the plane itself, but also against giants, dragons, and other terrible creatures that thunder across Ysgard's vast terrain.

entries.name: Immortal Wrath (Optional Rule)
entries.description: Any creature, other than a construct or undead, that is killed by an attack or a spell while on Ysgard is restored to life at dawn the next day. The creature has all its hit points restored, and all conditions and afflictions it suffered before its death are removed.

entries.name: Limbo
entries.description: A plane of pure chaos, a roiling soup of permanent matter and energy. Stone melts into water that freezes into metal, then turns into diamond that burns up into smoke that becomes snow, and on and on in an endless, unpredictable process of change. Fragments of more ordinary landscapes—bits of forest, meadow, ruined castles, and even burbling streams—drift through the disorder. The whole plane is a nightmarish riot.
rules: Creatures float in place due to the absence of gravity. A creature can move up to its walking speed in any direction by merely thinking of the desired direction of travel.

Disciplined and powerful minds can create islands within the plane, sometimes maintaining them for years. Simpleminded creatures may have less than a minute before their surroundings freeze, vanish, or turn to glass.

OPTIONAL RULE: POWER OF THE MIND
- As an action, a creature can make an Intelligence check to mentally move an object (DC 5-25 based on size) up to 5 feet + 1 foot per point by which the check exceeds the DC.
- Alter nonmagical objects (DC 10-25 based on size) into another nonliving form of the same size.
- Stabilize a spherical area (DC 5 + 5 per 10 feet radius) to prevent alteration for 24 hours.

entries.name: Pandemonium
entries.description: A plane of madness, a great mass of rock riddled with tunnels carved by howling winds. It is cold, noisy, and dark with no natural light. Wind quickly extinguishes nonmagical open flames and makes conversation possible only by yelling to a maximum distance of 10 feet. Creatures have disadvantage on ability checks that rely on hearing. Inhabitants are banished creatures driven mad by the winds or forced to take shelter in areas where the winds die down.
rules: Wind extinguishes nonmagical open flames (torches, campfires).
Conversation requires yelling, with a maximum distance of 10 feet.
Creatures have disadvantage on ability checks that rely on hearing.

entities.name: Optional Rule: Mad Winds
entities.canonicalText: This is a narrative rule description, not a discrete game entity. It outlines a conditional effect but lacks the structured formatting of a formal rule entry.

entities.name: The Abyss
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a plane of existence (The Abyss) but contains narrative exposition and rule mechanics rather than a discrete game entity. It includes optional rules but is not a standalone entity like a monster, item, or class.

entities.name: Abyssal Corruption
entities.canonicalText: The text describes the effects of the ABYSSAL CORRUPTION mechanic, which is a rule description rather than a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Important Layers
entities.canonicalText: The text describes multiple layers of the Abyss, each ruled by a demon lord, but the heading 'IMPORTANT LAYERS' is a section title rather than a discrete game entity.

entries.name: Carceri
entries.description: The model for all other prisons in existence, Carceri is a plane of desolation and despair. Its six layers hold vast bogs, fetid jungles, windswept deserts, jagged mountains, frigid oceans, and black ice. All form a miserable home for the traitors and backstabbers that are trapped on this prison plane.

layers.name: Vast Bogs
layers.description: A swampy layer filled with stagnant water and decaying vegetation.

layers.name: Fetid Jungles
layers.description: A dense, humid jungle teeming with dangerous flora and fauna.

layers.name: Windswept Deserts
layers.description: An arid expanse of shifting sands and relentless winds.

layers.name: Jagged Mountains
layers.description: A treacherous terrain of sharp peaks and perilous cliffs.

layers.name: Frigid Oceans
layers.description: A frozen sea of ice and snow-covered waters.

layers.name: Black Ice
layers.description: A glacial layer with deadly, unyielding ice formations.

features: A prison plane for traitors and backstabbers

entries.name: Carceri
entries.description: No one can leave Carceri easily. Magical efforts to leave the plane by any spell other than a wish simply fail. Portals and gates that open onto the plane become one-way only. Secret ways out of the plane exist, but they are hidden and well guarded by traps and deadly monsters.

entries: Hades is a negative plane of existence known as the Three Glooms, a gray land with an ashen sky devoid of joy, hope, or passion. Souls unclaimed by the gods of the Upper Planes or fiendish rulers of the Lower Planes become larvae, existing in a state of eternal desolation without sun, moon, stars, or seasons. {@rule Shadowfell Despair} can represent a visitor's despair.

entries.name: Gehenna
entries.description: The plane of suspicion and greed. It is the birthplace of the yugoloths, which dwell here in great numbers.
features: Volcanic mountains dominate each of the four layers
Rocky slopes with at least 45-degree inclines
Volcanic fissures venting noxious fumes or searing flames
entries.notable_traits: No room for mercy or compassion; inhabitants are among the greediest and most selfish in the multiverse

entities.name: Optional Rule: Cruel Hindrance
entities.canonicalText: The text contains a rule description but is not a discrete game entity. It includes narrative context about the Nine Hells and a rule about spellcasting on a plane, but the rule is presented as part of a larger descriptive passage rather than a standalone entity.

entities.name: Optional Rule: Pervasive Evil
entities.canonicalText: This is a narrative rule description, not a discrete game entity. It outlines a conditional effect but does not represent a standalone entity like a creature, item, or feat.

entities.name: THE NINE LAYERs
entities.canonicalText: The text describes the Nine Hells as a planar location with multiple layers, but it is not a single discrete game entity. It serves as a narrative overview of a planar region rather than a distinct entity like a creature, item, or rule.

entities.name: Acheron
entities.canonicalText: This is a descriptive passage about the plane of Acheron, not a discrete game entity with defined rules or statistics. It serves as narrative worldbuilding rather than a structured rule entry.

entities.name: Optional Rule: Bloodlust
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a rule but is presented as part of a narrative explanation rather than a discrete game entity with structured mechanics. It lacks the formal rule formatting (e.g., name, effect, prerequisites) required for a distinct entity.

entries.name: Mechanus
entries.description: A realm of clockwork gears, interlocked and turning according to measure. It embodies absolute order, with law reflected in its vast, unfathomable calculations. The plane's influence extends to those who dwell here.

entities.name: Optional Rule: Law of Averages
entities.canonicalText: This is a narrative rule description, not a discrete game entity. It explains a specific game mechanic but does not represent a separate entity like a spell, feat, or class.

entities.name: Arcadia
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a plane of existence (Arcadia) but contains narrative description rather than structured rule data. It includes lore about the plane's features and an optional rule, but no discrete game entity with explicit stat-block or rule-based parameters.

entities.name: Other Planes
entities.canonicalText: The text is a general narrative statement about the existence of realms between or beyond other planes, not a discrete game entity.

entries.name: The Outlands
entries.description: The plane between the Outer Planes. A plane of neutrality incorporating aspects of all planes in paradoxical balance. Features varied terrain including prairies, mountains, and shallow rivers.
entries.significance: Serves as a microcosm of the planes and the closest the Outer Planes come to resembling a Material Plane. Contains gate-towns connecting to Outer Planes.

entries.name: Sigil
entries.description: The city in the Outlands, often referred to as the 'City of Doors.' Located at the center of the circular Outlands plane.
entries.significance: Serves as a hub for planar travel and home to the gate-towns. Known for its chaotic yet balanced nature and planar emissaries.

entities.name: Gate-Towns of the Outlands
entities.canonicalText: Narrative description of a planar city

entries.name: Demiplanes
entries.content: Demiplanes are extradimensional spaces that come into being by a variety of means and boast their own physical laws. Some are created by spells. Others exist naturally, as folds of reality pinched off from the rest of the multiverse. Theoretically, a plane shift spell can carry travelers to a demiplane, but the proper frequency required for the tuning fork would be extremely hard to acquire. The gate spell is more reliable, assuming the caster knows of the demiplane. A demiplane can be as small as a single chamber or large enough to contain an entire realm. For example, a Mordenkainen's magnificent mansion spell creates a demiplane consisting of a foyer with multiple adjoining rooms, while the land of Barovia (in the Ravenloft setting) exists entirely within a demiplane under the sway of its vampire lord, Strahd von Zarovich. When a demiplane is connected to the Material Plane or some other plane, entering it can be as simple as stepping through a portal or passing through a wall of mist.

entries.name: The Far Realm
entries.description: A plane outside the known multiverse, possibly an entirely separate universe with its own physical and magical laws. Stray energies from this plane warp matter into alien shapes defying geometry and biology. Entities here are alien, with titanic creatures and unspeakable things that whisper awful truths. Mortals struggle to comprehend it, and some warlocks form pacts with its entities.

properties.portals: No well-known portals exist, though ancient elves once opened one at Firestorm Peak. Its location and home world are forgotten. Lost portals may still exist, marked by alien magic that mutates surrounding areas.

entities.name: Known Worlds of the Material Plane
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading listing known worlds of the Material Plane, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Traps
entities.canonicalText: The text describes trap mechanics and types (mechanical/magical) but does not represent a discrete game entity like a specific trap, creature, or item.

entities.name: Traps in Play
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading from the Dungeon Master's Guide explaining general trap mechanics, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: TRIGGERING A TRAP
entities.canonicalText: The text describes general mechanics for trap triggering rather than a specific discrete game entity.

entities.name: DETECTING AND DISABLING A TRAP
entities.canonicalText: The text describes general rules and mechanics for detecting and disarming traps, not a specific discrete game entity.

entities.name: Trap Effects
entities.canonicalText: This is a general explanation of trap mechanics and severity levels, not a specific discrete game entity like a spell, item, or monster.

entities.name: Sample Traps
entities.canonicalText: This is a section introduction describing the contents of the 'Sample Traps' section, not a discrete game entity.

entries.description: A mechanical trap using a trip wire to collapse unstable ceiling supports. The trip wire is 3 inches off the ground between support beams. DC 10 to spot, DC 15 Dexterity check (thieves' tools) to disable. Failed check triggers collapse. Inspecting beams reveals they are wedged in place. Knocking over a beam with an action also triggers the trap.
trigger: Trip wire activation (DC 15 Dexterity check with thieves' tools; disadvantage with edged weapon/tool)
Knocking over a support beam with an action
effect: Unstable ceiling collapses, dealing 22 (4d10) bludgeoning damage on failed DC 15 Dexterity saving throw (half on success)
Area becomes difficult terrain after collapse

entries.description: A failed DC 15 Dexterity check using thieves' tools (or any edged weapon/tool with disadvantage) to break the trip wire.

The net covers a 10-foot-square area. Creatures in the area are restrained and knocked prone on a DC 10 Strength saving throw. A creature can use its action to make a DC 10 Strength check, freeing itself or another creature within reach on a success.
The net has AC 10 and 20 hit points. 5 slashing damage to the net (AC 10) destroys a 5-foot-square section, freeing any creature trapped there.

damageInflict: half as much damage on a successful save
conditionInflict: more than 20 pounds of weight on the pressure plate

entities.name: Mechanical trap
entities.canonicalText: section heading

entries.name: Poison Darts Mechanical Trap
entries.trigger: A creature steps on a hidden pressure plate.
entries.effect: Poison-tipped darts shoot from spring-loaded or pressurized tubes embedded in walls. The trap activates when more than 20 pounds of weight is placed on the pressure plate, releasing four darts. Each dart makes a ranged attack with a +8 bonus against a random target within 10 feet. A hit deals 2 (1d4) piercing damage and requires a DC 15 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the target takes 11 (2d10) poison damage; on a successful save, they take half damage.

entities.name: POISON NEEDLE Mechanical trap
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a mechanical trap's rules but is part of a larger rulebook section. It contains narrative and rule text rather than a discrete game entity.

entities.name: ROLLING SPHERE Mechanical trap
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a mechanical trap's mechanics but is part of a larger rulebook section. It contains narrative and rule details but isn't a discrete game entity like a monster, item, or class.

entities.name: Chapter 7: Treasure
entities.canonicalText: This is a chapter heading and introductory text, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Random Treasure
entities.canonicalText: This text describes a section's purpose (generating treasure tables) rather than a specific game entity like an item, spell, or monster.

entities.name: Treasure Tables
entities.canonicalText: This is a heading and explanatory text for treasure tables, not a discrete game entity. It describes rules for using treasure tables but does not represent a specific entity like a monster, item, or feat.

entities.name: Using the Treasure Hoard Tables
entities.canonicalText: This text describes procedural guidelines for using treasure hoard tables, not a discrete game entity. It explains mechanics for determining hoard contents based on challenge ratings and provides usage instructions, not a specific item, creature, or rule feature.

entities.name: Gemstones
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading explaining treasure distribution rules, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Art Objects
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading explaining treasure generation rules, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Magic Items
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading introducing the concept of magic items, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Rarity
entities.canonicalText: The text describes the concept of rarity as a game mechanic but does not represent a discrete entity. It is narrative explaining rules rather than defining a specific game object.

entities.name: Buying and Selling
entities.canonicalText: The text provides narrative guidelines for DMs about magic item economy and is not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: IDENTIFYING A MAGIC ITEM
entities.canonicalText: This text describes rules and procedures for identifying magic items, not a discrete game entity.

entries: Attunement is a bond required for some magic items to function. Prerequisites for attunement may specify a class, in which case the creature must be a member of that class (monsters qualify if they have spell slots and use the class's spell list).
A creature gains only nonmagical benefits of an item without attunement, unless stated otherwise (e.g., a magic shield provides normal shield benefits to non-attuned users).

Attuning to an item requires a short rest focused on the item while in physical contact. The rest cannot be used to learn the item's properties. If interrupted, attunement fails. Success grants intuitive understanding of the item's magical properties, including command words.

An item can be attuned to only one creature at a time. A creature can be attuned to no more than three magic items. Attempting to attune to a fourth item fails; the creature must end an existing attunement first. A creature cannot attune to multiple copies of the same item.

Attunement ends if the creature no longer meets prerequisites, if the item has been more than 100 feet away for 24 hours, if the creature dies, or if another creature attunes to the item. A creature can voluntarily end attunement by spending another short rest focused on the item, unless the item is cursed.

entities.name: Cursed Items
entities.canonicalText: This text describes rule mechanics for cursed items rather than a discrete game entity.

entries: Each magic item belongs to a category: armor, potions, rings, rods, scrolls, staffs, wands, weapons, or wondrous items.

entities.name: Armor
entities.canonicalText: This text describes general rules about armor functionality and type determination, not a discrete game entity.

entries.name: Potions
entries.description: Different kinds of magical liquids are grouped in the category of potions: brews made from enchanted herbs, water from magical fountains or sacred springs, and oils that are applied to a creature or object. Most potions consist of one ounce of liquid.
entries.usage: Drinking a potion or administering a potion to another character requires an action. Applying an oil might take longer, as specified in its description.
entries.effects: Once used, a potion takes effect immediately, and it is used up.

entities.name: RINGs
entities.canonicalText: This is a general rule about magic rings, not a specific discrete game entity.

entities.name: Rods
entities.canonicalText: This is a general description of magic rods' physical form, not a specific discrete game entity. It serves as a rule clarification rather than defining a unique item or concept.

entries.name: Scrolls
entries.description: Most scrolls are spells stored in written form, while a few bear unique incantations that produce potent wards. A scroll is a roll of paper (sometimes attached to wooden rods) typically kept in a tube of ivory, jade, leather, metal, or wood.
entries.usage: A scroll is a consumable magic item. Unleashing its magic requires using an action to read the scroll. Once used, the scroll cannot be used again (its words fade or it crumbles into dust).

entries.text: A magic staff is about 5 or 6 feet long. Staffs vary widely in appearance: some are of nearly equal diameter throughout and smooth, others are gnarled and twisted, some are made of wood, and others are composed of polished metal or crystal. Depending on the material, a staff weighs between 2 and 7 pounds.
Unless a staff's description says otherwise, a staff can be used as a quarterstaff.

entries.name: Description
entries.value: A magic wand is about 15 inches long and crafted of metal, bone, or wood. It is tipped with metal, crystal stone, or some other material.

entities.name: Weapons
entities.canonicalText: This is a section heading introducing the topic of magic weapons, not a specific discrete game entity.

entities.name: Variant: Mixing Potions
entities.canonicalText: This is a rule description about potion interactions, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: Potion Miscibility

entries: Wondrous items include worn items such as boots, belts, capes, gloves, and various pieces of jewelry and decoration, such as amulets, brooches, and circlets. Bags, carpets, crystal balls, figurines, horns, musical instruments, and other objects also fall into this catch-all category.

entries: While you're wearing this armor, any critical hit against you becomes a normal hit.

This ceramic jug appears to be able to hold a gallon of liquid and weighs 12 pounds whether full or empty. Sloshing sounds can be heard from within the jug when it is shaken, even if the jug is empty.

You can use an action and name one liquid from the table below to cause the jug to produce the chosen liquid. Afterward, you can uncork the jug as an action and pour that liquid out, up to 2 gallons per minute. The maximum amount of liquid the jug can produce depends on the liquid you named.
Once the jug starts producing a liquid, it can't produce a different one, or more of one that has reached its maximum, until the next dawn.

entries.name: Amulet of Health
entries: Your Constitution score is 19 while you wear this amulet. It has no effect on you if your Constitution is already 19 or higher.

entities.name: Amulet of Proof Against Detection and Location

entries: While wearing this amulet, you are hidden from divination magic. You can't be targeted by such magic or perceived through magical scrying sensors.
While wearing this amulet, you can use an action to name a location that you are familiar with on another plane of existence. Then make a {@dc 15} Intelligence check.
On a successful check, you cast the {@spell Plane Shift|PHB} spell.
On a failure, you and each creature and object within 15 feet of you travel to a random destination. Roll a d100.
On a 1–60, you travel to a random location on the plane you named. On a 61–100, you travel to a randomly determined plane of existence.

entities.name: Wondrous item, leģendary
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but is incomplete and contains narrative elements. The 'Deck of Many Things' is a legendary item, but the source text is cut off mid-description and includes narrative effects rather than structured rules. The schema requires complete, discrete entity data which is not fully provided here.

entities.name: Armor Class: 20
entities.canonicalText: Source text describes creature statistics (Hit Points, Speed) but does not mention Armor Class or any other discrete game entity.

entities.name: Damage Immunities: poison, psychic
entities.canonicalText: Part of a stat block for a siege tower, not a discrete entity

entries: Armor (light, medium, or heavy), rare (+1), very rare (+2), or legendary (+3). You have a bonus to AC while wearing this armor. The bonus is determined by its rarity.
You have resistance to nonmagical damage while you wear this armor.

You can use an action to make yourself immune to nonmagical damage for 10 minutes or until you are no longer wearing the armor. Once this special action is used, it can't be used again until the next dawn.
You have resistance to one type of damage while you wear this armor. The DM chooses the type or determines it randomly from the options below.

variants.name: Armor of Resistance
variants.description: Light, medium, or heavy armor

entities.name: Armor (plate), rare (requires attunement)
entities.canonicalText: This is descriptive text for an armor item, not a discrete game entity. The text explains properties and effects of the armor rather than defining a separate entity.

entries.name: Armor (Shield)
entries.description: You gain a +2 bonus to AC against ranged attacks while you wield this shield. This bonus is in addition to the shield's normal bonus to AC. In addition, whenever an attacker makes a ranged attack against a target within 5 feet of you, you can use your reaction to become the target of the attack instead.

entries.name: Arrow of Slaying
entries.description: When a creature of the targeted type, race, or group takes damage from this arrow, it must make a DC 17 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, it takes an extra 6d10 piercing damage, or half as much on a successful save. After dealing its extra damage, the arrow becomes nonmagical.

entities.name: Wondrous item, rare
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but is presented as a rule entry rather than a discrete entity. It contains narrative and mechanical details about an item's function, which would be classified as a 'wondrousItem' if structured as a stat block, but the provided schema requires explicit entity types for items.

entities.name: d100 Effect
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a list of random effects triggered by a d100 roll, which are not individual discrete game entities but rather part of a procedural system for generating events.

entries: This bag superficially resembles a bag of holding but is a feeding orifice for a gigantic extradimensional creature. Turning the bag inside out closes the orifice.

The extradimensional creature attached to the bag can sense whatever is placed inside the bag. Animal or vegetable matter placed wholly in the bag is devoured and lost forever. When part of a living creature is placed in the bag, as happens when someone reaches inside it, there is a 50 percent chance that the creature is pulled inside the bag.

A creature inside the bag can use its action to try to escape with a successful {@dc 15} Strength check. Another creature can use its action to reach into the bag to pull a creature out, doing so with a successful {@dc 20} Strength check (provided it isn't pulled inside the bag first). Any creature that starts its turn inside the bag is devoured, its body destroyed.

Inanimate objects can be stored in the bag, which can hold a cubic foot of such material. However, once each day, the bag swallows any objects inside it and spits them out into another plane of existence. The DM determines the time and plane.
If the bag is pierced or torn, it is destroyed, and anything contained within it is transported to a random location on the Astral Plane.

entities.name: Wondrous item, uncommon
entities.canonicalText: The text describes multiple items and mechanics, including a bag of holding and a bag that generates creatures, but does not present a single discrete game entity with consistent properties. It contains narrative descriptions and rules rather than a unified entity.

entries: This small black sphere measures 3/4 of an inch in diameter and weighs an ounce. Typically, 1d4 + 4 beads of force are found together.

You can use an action to throw the bead up to 60 feet. The bead explodes on impact and is destroyed. Each creature within a 10-foot radius of where the bead landed must succeed on a DC 15 Dexterity saving throw or take 5d4 force damage. A sphere of transparent force then encloses the area for 1 minute. Any creature that failed the save and is completely within the area is trapped inside this sphere. Creatures that succeeded on the save, or are partially within the area, are pushed away from the center of the sphere until they are no longer inside it. Only breathable air can pass through the sphere's wall. No attack or other effect can.

An enclosed creature can use its action to push against the sphere's wall, moving the sphere up to half the creature's walking speed. The sphere can be picked up, and its magic causes it to weigh only 1 pound, regardless of the weight of creatures inside.
Your Constitution score increases by 2, to a maximum of 20.
You have advantage on Charisma (Persuasion) checks made to interact with dwarves.
While attuned to the belt, you have a 50 percent chance each day at dawn of growing a full beard if you're capable of growing one, or a visibly thicker beard if you already have one.
If you aren't a dwarf, you gain the following additional benefits while wearing the belt: You have advantage on saving throws against poison, and you have resistance against poison damage.
You have darkvision out to a range of 60 feet.
You can speak, read, and write Dwarvish.

entries.name: Belt of Giant Strength
entries: While wearing this belt, your Strength score changes to a score granted by the belt. If your Strength is already equal to or greater than the belt's score, the item has no effect on you.
Six varieties of this belt exist, corresponding with and having rarity according to the six kinds of true giants.
The belt of stone giant strength and the belt of frost giant strength look different, but they have the same effect.
You gain a +1 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.
Your hit point maximum increases by 1 for each level you have attained.

{@condition curse} This axe is cursed, and becoming attuned to it extends the curse to you. As long as you remain cursed, you are unwilling to part with the axe, keeping it within reach at all times. You also have disadvantage on attack rolls with weapons other than this one, unless no foe is within 60 feet of you that you can see or hear.

Whenever a hostile creature damages you while the axe is in your possession, you must succeed on a {@dc 15} Wisdom saving throw or go berserk. While berserk, you must use your action each round to attack the creature nearest to you with the axe. If you can make extra attacks as part of the Attack action, you use those extra attacks, moving to attack the next nearest creature after you fell your current target. If you have multiple possible targets, you attack one at random. You are berserk until you start your turn with no creatures within 60 feet of you that you can see or hear.
While you wear these boots, your steps make no sound, regardless of the surface you are moving across. You also have advantage on Dexterity (Stealth) checks that rely on moving silently.

entries.name: Boots of Levitation
entries: While you wear these boots, you can use an action to cast the levitate spell on yourself at will.

While you wear these boots, you can use a bonus action and click the boots' heels together. If you do, the boots double your walking speed, and any creature that makes an opportunity attack against you has disadvantage on the attack roll. If you click your heels together again, you end the effect.
When the boots' property has been used for a total of 10 minutes, the magic ceases to function until you finish a long rest.

entities.name: BOOTS OF STRIDING AND SPRINGING Wondrous item, uncommon (requires attunement)

entries.name: Striding and Springing
entries.description: While you wear these boots, your walking speed becomes 30 feet, unless your walking speed is higher, and your speed isn't reduced if you are encumbered or wearing heavy armor. In addition, you can jump three times the normal distance, though you can't jump farther than your remaining movement would allow.

entries.name: Boots of the Winterlands
entries: You have resistance to cold damage.
You ignore difficult terrain created by ice or snow.
You can tolerate temperatures as low as -50 degrees Fahrenheit without any additional protection. If you wear heavy clothes, you can tolerate temperatures as low as -100 degrees Fahrenheit.

entities.name: BOWL OF COMMANDING WATER ELEMENTALS Wondrous item, rare
entities.canonicalText: The source text describes a wondrous item but does not present it as a discrete entity in the schema. The content is narrative rules text for an item, not a standalone entity.

entries: While wearing these bracers, you have proficiency with the longbow and shortbow, and you gain a +2 bonus to damage rolls on ranged attacks made with such weapons.
While wearing these bracers, you gain a +2 bonus to AC if you are wearing no armor and using no shield.

entities.name: BRAZIER OF COMMANDING FIRE ELEMENTALS Wondrous item, rare
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but lacks required structured fields like 'name', 'rarity', 'weight', and 'properties' needed for the 'WondrousItem' schema. The source text is a narrative description rather than a fully structured entry.

entries: While wearing this brooch, you have resistance to force damage.
You have immunity to damage from the {@item magic missile|PHB} spell.

This slender taper is dedicated to a deity and shares that deity's alignment. The candle's alignment can be detected with the detect evil and good spell. The DM chooses the god and associated alignment or determines the alignment randomly.

Lighting the candle requires an action. After burning for 4 hours, the candle is destroyed. You can snuff it out early for use at a later time. Deduct the time it burned in increments of 1 minute from the candle's total burn time.

While lit, the candle sheds dim light in a 30-foot radius. Any creature within that light whose alignment matches that of the candle makes attack rolls, saving throws, and ability checks with advantage. In addition, a cleric or druid in the light whose alignment matches the candle's can cast 1st-level spells he or she has prepared without expending spell slots, though the spell's effect is as if cast with a 1st-level slot.
Alternatively, when you light the candle for the first time, you can cast the gate spell with it. Doing so destroys the candle.
While wearing this cape, you can use it to cast {@spell dimension door} as an action. This property can't be used again until the next dawn.

When you disappear, you leave behind a cloud of smoke, and you appear in a similar cloud of smoke at your destination. The smoke lightly obscures the space you left and the space you appear in, and it dissipates at the end of your next turn. A light or stronger wind disperses the smoke.

entities.name: Wondrous item, very rare
entities.canonicalText: This is descriptive text for a wondrous item, not a discrete game entity. The content explains mechanics and rules rather than defining a separate entity.

entities.name: CENSER OF CONTROLLING AIR ELEMENTALS Wondrous item, rare

entries.name: Censer of Controlling Air Elementals
entries.description: While incense is burning in this censer, you can use an action to speak the censer's command word and summon an air elemental, as if you had cast the conjure elemental spell. The censer can't be used this way again until the next dawn. This 6-inch-wide, 1-foot-high vessel resembles a chalice with a decorated lid.

entries.name: Cloak of Web Walking
properties: Climbing speed equal to walking speed
Move up/down/across vertical surfaces and upside down on ceilings (hands free)
Cannot be caught in webs; move through webs as difficult terrain
Use an action to cast web (save DC 13) with doubled area; cooldown until next dawn

entities.name: Wondrous item, rare (requires attunement)
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but is part of a rule description rather than a discrete entity entry. It lacks a formal item name and is presented as a rule effect, not a standalone entity.

entries.name: Pipes of the Swarming Rats
entries.description: You must be proficient with wind instruments to use these pipes. While attuned, ordinary and giant rats ignore you unless threatened. The pipes have 3 charges. Playing them as an action allows expending 1-3 charges (via bonus action) to summon rat swarms (per charge, as determined by DM). Swarms move toward the music but aren't controlled otherwise. Charges regenerate 1d3 daily at dawn. When a non-controlled rat swarm enters 30ft while playing, you can make a Charisma check contested by the swarm's Wisdom. Losing means the swarm ignores the pipes for 24h; winning makes it friendly (obeying commands or defending itself if uncommanded) until the music stops. Friendly swarms lose control if they can't hear the pipes' music on their turn.

entities.name: Wondrous item, legendary (requires attunement)
entities.canonicalText: The text describes the functionality of a wondrous item (Cloak of Invisibility) but does not present it as a discrete entity with a dedicated entry in the rulebook. It is part of an item's description rather than a standalone rule or entity.

entries: You gain a +1 bonus to AC and saving throws while you wear this cloak.

entities.name: CLOAK OF THE MANTA RAY Wondrous item, uncommon
entities.canonicalText: The source text describes properties of the Cloak of the Manta Ray wondrous item, but the provided entity name already includes the item's name and rarity. The schema requires explicit fields for name, itemCategory, rarity, and properties, which are not fully captured in the source text. The page number (159) is also not included in the schema.

entries: Wondrous item, very rare or legendary (requires attunement)
The typical crystal ball, a very rare item, is about 6 inches in diameter. While touching it, you can cast the scrying spell (save DC 17) with it.

variants.name: Crystal Ball of Mind Reading
variants.description: You can use an action to cast the detect thoughts spell (save DC 17) while you are scrying with the crystal ball, targeting creatures you can see within 30 feet of the spell's sensor. You don't need to concentrate on this detect thoughts to maintain it during its duration, but it ends if scrying ends.

variants.name: Crystal Ball of Telepathy
variants.description: While scrying with the crystal ball, you can communicate telepathically with creatures you can see within 30 feet of the spell's sensor. You can also use an action to cast the suggestion spell (save DC 17) through the sensor on one of those creatures. You don't need to concentrate on this suggestion to maintain it during its duration, but it ends if scrying ends. Once used, the suggestion power of the crystal ball can't be used again until the next dawn.

variants.name: Crystal Ball of True Seeing
variants.description: While scrying with the crystal ball, you have truesight with a radius of 120 feet centered on the spell's sensor.

entities.name: Cube of Force Faces
entities.canonicalText: The source text does not mention 'CUBE OF FORCE FACES' and instead describes a cube's charge mechanics as part of a rule explanation, not a discrete game entity.

entities.name: DAERN's INSTANT FORTRESS Wondrous item, rare
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but contains narrative and rule text mixed with formatting that cannot be cleanly parsed into a single structured entity. It includes activation mechanics, damage effects, and durability rules that span multiple categories.

properties.name: Magic Property
properties.value: +1 bonus to attack and damage rolls

properties.name: Poison Coating
properties.value: You can use an action to coat the blade with poison. The poison lasts for 1 minute or until an attack using this weapon hits a creature. On a failed DC 15 Constitution saving throw, the target takes 2d10 poison damage and is poisoned for 1 minute. The dagger cannot be used this way again until the next dawn.

entities.name: DECANTER OF ENDLESS WATER Wondrous item, uncommon
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but is part of a rulebook entry that includes both item description and ability mechanics. The full rule entry requires structured formatting beyond a single entity type, including multiple components (item properties, usage, options, and effects).

entries: The Deck of Many Things is a magical artifact that grants its owner a deck of 25 cards, each with unique effects. Drawing specific cards can alter the game's narrative and mechanics, including earning enmity, gaining XP, summoning entities, or altering reality. The following are the effects of individual cards:
{@list ['Flames', 'Rogue', 'Euryale', 'The Fates', 'Fool', 'Gem', 'Idiot', 'Jester', 'Key', 'Knight', 'Moon', 'Ruin', 'Skull', 'Star', 'Sun']}
{@item Deck of Many Things|DMG} is a magic item that grants its owner a deck of 25 cards with various effects. The cards include:
{@list ['Flames', 'Rogue', 'Euryale', 'The Fates', 'Fool', 'Gem', 'Idiot', 'Jester', 'Key', 'Knight', 'Moon', 'Ruin', 'Skull', 'Star', 'Sun']}
Each card's effect is triggered when drawn, with some requiring specific conditions (e.g., 'Flames' creates an enemy devil, 'Rogue' introduces a secret enemy, 'Skull' summons an avatar of death).

entities.name: AVATAR OF DEATH Medium undead, neutral evi

hp.formula: half the hit point maximum of its summoner

languages: all languages known to its summoner

trait.name: Incorporeal Movement
entries: The avatar can move through other creatures and objects as if they were difficult terrain. It takes 5 (1d10) force damage if it ends its turn inside an object.
You gain a +3 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.

The first time you attack with the sword on each of your turns, you can transfer some or all of the sword's bonus to your Armor Class, instead of using the bonus on any attacks that turn. For example, you could reduce the bonus to your attack and damage rolls to +1 and gain a +2 bonus to AC. The adjusted bonuses remain in effect until the start of your next turn, although you must hold the sword to gain a bonus to AC from it.

entities.name: Armor (plate), very rare (requires attunement)
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a cursed armor item but does not present it as a discrete game entity with a dedicated stat block. It is part of a narrative description within a rulebook section about cursed items.

entities.name: DIMENSIONAL SHACKLES Wondrous item, rare
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but is part of a rule description rather than a discrete entity entry. The structure follows a rulebook format with narrative rules, not a standalone entity definition.

entries: While wearing this armor, you gain a +1 bonus to AC, you have advantage on saving throws against the Frightful Presence and breath weapons of dragons, and you have resistance to one damage type that is determined by the kind of dragon that provided the scales (see the table).

Additionally, you can focus your senses as an action to magically discern the distance and direction to the closest dragon within 30 miles of you that is of the same type as the armor. This special action can't be used again until the next dawn.

entities.name: DUST OF DISAPPEARANCE Wondrous item, uncommon
DUST OF SNEEZING AND CHOKING Wondrous item, uncommon

entries.name: Dust of Sneezing and Choking
entries.description: Found in a small container, this powder resembles very fine sand. It appears to be dust of disappearance, and an identify spell reveals it to be such. There is enough of it for one use.

usage.effect: You and each creature that needs to breathe within 30 feet of you must succeed on a DC 15 Constitution saving throw or become unable to breathe, while sneezing uncontrollably. A creature affected in this way is incapacitated and suffocating.
usage.duration: 1 round (until the creature succeeds on a saving throw or uses the Lesser Restoration spell)

remedy: Repeating the saving throw at the end of each turn

entries: While wearing this armor, you gain a +2 bonus to AC. In addition, if an effect moves you against your will along the ground, you can use your reaction to reduce the distance you are moved by up to 10 feet.
You gain a +3 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.
It has the thrown property with a normal range of 20 feet and a long range of 60 feet.
When you hit with a ranged attack using this weapon, it deals an extra {@damage 1d8} damage or, if the target is a giant, {@damage 2d8} damage.
Immediately after the attack, the weapon flies back to your hand.
While wearing this armor, you gain a +3 bonus to AC, you are immune to fire damage, and you can understand and speak Primordial.
In addition, you can stand on and walk across molten rock as if it were solid ground.

When you use an action to break the gem, an elemental is summoned as if you had cast the conjure elemental spell, and the gem's magic is lost. The type of gem determines the elemental summoned by the spell.
You gain a +1 bonus to AC while you wear this armor.
You are considered proficient with this armor even if you lack proficiency with medium armor.
Smoke leaks from the lead-stoppered mouth of this brass bottle, which weighs 1 pound.
When you use an action to remove the stopper, a cloud of thick smoke pours out in a 60-foot radius from the bottle. The cloud's area is heavily obscured.
Each minute the bottle remains open and within the cloud, the radius increases by 10 feet until it reaches its maximum radius of 120 feet.
The cloud persists as long as the bottle is open. Closing the bottle requires you to speak its command word as an action.

Once the bottle is closed, the cloud disperses after 10 minutes. A moderate wind (11 to 20 miles per hour) can also disperse the smoke after 1 minute, and a strong wind (21 or more miles per hour) can do so after 1 round.

These crystal lenses fit over the eyes. They have 3 charges. While wearing them, you can expend 1 charge as an action to cast {@spell charm person|PHB} (save {@dc 13}) on a humanoid within 30 feet of you, provided that you and the target can see each other. The lenses regain all expended charges daily at dawn.

These crystal lenses fit over the eyes. While wearing them, you can see much better than normal out to a range of 1 foot. You have advantage on Intelligence (Investigation) checks that rely on sight while searching an area or studying an object within that range.

entries.name: Description
entries: These crystal lenses fit over the eyes. While wearing them, you have advantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sight. In conditions of clear visibility, you can make out details of even extremely distant creatures and objects as small as 2 feet across.

entities.name: FIGURINE OF WONDROUS POWER Wondrous item, rarity by figurine
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but contains nested entries (Bronze Griffon, Ebony Fly) and procedural rules, making it a composite entry rather than a single discrete entity. The primary entry is a template with examples, not a standalone item.

entries: When you hit with an attack using this magic sword, the target takes an extra {@damage 1d6} cold damage.
While you hold the sword, you have resistance to fire damage.
In freezing temperatures, the blade sheds bright light in a 10-foot radius and dim light for an additional 10 feet.
When you draw this weapon, you can extinguish all nonmagical flames within 30 feet of you. This property can be used no more than once per hour.
Your Strength score is 19 while you wear these gauntlets. They have no effect on you if your Strength is already 19 or higher.
This prism has 50 charges. While you are holding it, you can use an action to speak one of three command words to cause one of the following effects:

• The first command word causes the gem to shed bright light in a 30-foot radius and dim light for an additional 30 feet. This effect doesn't expend a charge. It lasts until you use a bonus action to repeat the command word or until you use another function of the gem.

• The second command word expends 1 charge and causes the gem to fire a brilliant beam of light at one creature you can see within 60 feet of you. The creature must succeed on a {@dc 15} Constitution saving throw or become blinded for 1 minute. The creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

• The third command word expends 5 charges and causes the gem to flare with blinding light in a 30-foot cone originating from it. Each creature in the cone must make a {@dc 15} Constitution saving throw as if struck by the beam created with the second command word.
When all of the gem's charges are expended, the gem becomes a nonmagical jewel worth 50 gp.
You gain a +1 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.

When you hit a giant with it, the giant takes an extra {@damage 2d6} damage of the weapon's type and must succeed on a {@dc 15} Strength saving throw or fall {@condition prone}. For the purpose of this weapon, 'giant' refers to any creature with the giant type, including ettins and trolls.

entities.name: GLAMOURED STUDDED LEATHER Armor (studded leather), rare

abilities.name: Illusory Appearance
abilities.description: You can use a bonus action to speak the armor's command word and cause it to assume the appearance of a normal set of clothing or some other kind of armor. You decide what it looks like, including color, style, and accessories, but the armor retains its normal bulk and weight. The illusory appearance lasts until you use this property again or remove the armor.

entries: When a ranged weapon attack hits you while wearing these gloves, you can use your reaction to reduce the damage by {@damage 1d10} + your Dexterity modifier, provided you have a free hand. If you reduce the damage to 0, you can catch the missile if it is small enough for you to hold in that hand.

entities.name: GLOVES OF SWIMMING AND CLIMBING Wondrous item, uncommon (requires attunement)

entries.name: Gloves of Swimming and Climbing
effects: While wearing these gloves, climbing and swimming don't cost you extra movement
You gain a +5 bonus to Strength (Athletics) checks made to climb or swim

entries: While wearing these dark lenses, you have darkvision out to a range of 60 feet.
If you already have darkvision, wearing the goggles increases its range by 60 feet.
You gain a +1 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.

Giant's Bane (Requires Attunement). You must be wearing a belt of giant strength (any variety) and gauntlets of ogre power to attune to this weapon. The attunement ends if you take off either of those items. While you are attuned to this weapon and holding it, your Strength score increases by 4 and can exceed 20, but not 30. When you roll a 20 on an attack roll made with this weapon against a giant, the giant must succeed on a DC 17 Constitution saving throw or die.

The hammer also has 5 charges. While attuned to it, you can expend 1 charge and make a ranged weapon attack with the hammer, hurling it as if it had the thrown property with a normal range of 20 feet and a long range of 60 feet. If the attack hits, the hammer unleashes a thunderclap audible out to 300 feet. The target and every creature within 30 feet of it must succeed on a DC 17 Constitution saving throw or be {@condition stunned} until the end of your next turn. The hammer regains 1d4 + 1 expended charges daily at dawn.
While wearing this hat, you can use an action to cast the {@spell disguise self|PHB} spell from it at will. The spell ends if the hat is removed.
Your Intelligence score is 19 while you wear this headband. It has no effect on you if your Intelligence is already 19 or higher.

This dazzling helm is set with 1d10 diamonds, 2d10 rubies, 3d10 fire opals, and 4d10 opals. Any gem pried from the helm crumbles to dust. When all the gems are removed or destroyed, the helm loses its magic.

You can use an action to cast one of the following spells (save DC {@dc 18}), using one of the helm's gems of the specified type as a component: {@spell daylight} (opal), {@spell fireball} (fire opal), {@spell prismatic spray} (diamond), or {@spell wall of fire} (ruby). The gem is destroyed when the spell is cast and disappears from the helm.

As long as it has at least one diamond, the helm emits dim light in a 30-foot radius when at least one undead is within that area. Any undead that starts its turn in that area takes {@damage 1d6} radiant damage.
As long as the helm has at least one ruby, you have resistance to fire damage.

As long as the helm has at least one fire opal, you can use an action and speak a command word to cause one weapon you are holding to burst into flames. The flames emit bright light in a 10-foot radius and dim light for an additional 10 feet. The flames are harmless to you and the weapon. When you hit with an attack using the blazing weapon, the target takes an extra {@damage 1d6} fire damage. The flames last until you use a bonus action to speak the command word again or until you drop or stow the weapon.

Roll a d20 if you are wearing the helm and take fire damage as a result of failing a saving throw against a spell. On a roll of 1, the helm emits beams of light from its remaining gems. Each creature within 60 feet of the helm other than you must succeed on a DC {@dc 17} Dexterity saving throw or be struck by a beam, taking radiant damage equal to the number of gems in the helm. The helm and its gems are then destroyed.

While wearing this helm, you can use an action to cast the detect thoughts spell (save DC {@dc 13}) from it. As long as you maintain concentration on the spell, you can use a bonus action to send a telepathic message to a creature you are focused on. It can reply—using a bonus, action to do so—while your focus on it continues.

While focusing on a creature with detect thoughts, you can use an action to cast the suggestion spell (save DC {@dc 13}) from the helm on that creature. Once used, the suggestion property can't be used again until the next dawn.
This helm has 3 charges.
While wearing it, you can use an action and expend 1 charge to cast the {@spell phb.spell.teleport} spell from it.
The helm regains 1d3 expended charges daily at dawn.

entities.name: HEWARD's HaNDY HaVERSACK Wondrous item, rare

entries.name: He ward's Handy Haversack
limitations: Overloaded haversack ruptures and is destroyed
Sharp object piercing or tearing destroys the haversack
Destroyed haversack's contents are lost (artifacts reappear)
Turning inside out causes contents to spill; must be righted
Placing inside extradimensional space (bag of holding, etc.) creates an Astral Plane gate

entries: You gain a +3 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.
When you hit a fiend or an undead with it, that creature takes an extra {@damage 2d10} radiant damage.

While you hold the drawn sword, it creates an aura in a 10-foot radius around you. You and all creatures friendly to you in the aura have advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.
If you have 17 or more levels in the paladin class, the radius of the aura increases to 30 feet.

entities.name: HORN OF BLASTING Wondrous item, rare

entries.name: Horn of Blasting
entries.effect: You can use an action to speak the horn's command word and then blow the horn, which emits a thunderous blast in a 30-foot cone that is audible 600 feet away. Each creature in the cone must make a DC 15 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, a creature takes 5d6 thunder damage and is deafened for 1 minute. On a successful save, a creature takes half as much damage and isn't deafened. Creatures and objects made of glass or crystal have disadvantage on the saving throw and take 10d6 thunder damage instead of 5d6. Each use of the horn's magic has a 20 percent chance of causing the horn to explode. The explosion deals 10d6 fire damage to the blower and destroys the horn.

entries: You can use an action to blow this horn. In response, warrior spirits from the plane of Ysgard appear within 60 feet of you. These spirits use the berserker statistics from the Monster Manual. They return to Ysgard after 1 hour or when they drop to 0 hit points. Once you use the horn, it can't be used again until 7 days have passed.

Four types of horn of Valhalla are known to exist, each made of a different metal. The horn's type determines how many berserkers answer its summons, as well as the requirement for its use. The DM chooses the horn's type or determines it randomly.
If you blow the horn without meeting its requirement, the summoned berserkers attack you. If you meet the requirement, they are friendly to you and your companions and follow your commands.

variants.name: Silver or Brass (Rare)
variants.description: The horn's type determines how many berserkers answer its summons, as well as the requirement for its use.

variants.name: Bronze (Very Rare)
variants.description: The horn's type determines how many berserkers answer its summons, as well as the requirement for its use.

variants.name: Iron (Legendary)
variants.description: The horn's type determines how many berserkers answer its summons, as well as the requirement for its use.

entries: While all four shoes are affixed to the hooves of a horse or similar creature, they allow the creature to move normally while floating 4 inches above the ground.
This effect means the creature can cross or stand above nonsolid or unstable surfaces, such as water or lava.
The creature leaves no tracks and ignores difficult terrain.
In addition, the creature can move at normal speed for up to 12 hours a day without suffering exhaustion from a forced march.

entities.name: HORSESHOES OF SPEED Wondrous item, rare
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but lacks required structural elements like name, type, rarity, and properties. The provided entity name is incomplete (missing 'Wondrous Item' classification) and the source text doesn't contain explicit property descriptions.

entries: An instrument of the bards is an exquisite example of its kind, superior to an ordinary instrument in every way. Seven types of these instruments exist, each named after a legendary bard college.
A creature that attempts to play the instrument without being attuned to it must succeed on a {@dc 15} Wisdom saving throw or take {@damage 2d3} psychic damage.

You can use an action to play the instrument and cast one of its spells. Once the instrument has been used to cast a spell, it can't be used to cast that spell again until the next dawn. The spells use your spellcasting ability and spell save DC.

When you use the instrument to cast a spell that causes targets to become charmed on a failed save, the targets have disadvantage on the saving throw. This effect applies whether you are using the instrument as the source of the spell or as a spellcasting focus.

entities.name: IROn BaNds OF BILarro Wondrous item, rare
entities.canonicalText: The text describes a wondrous item but lacks a discrete entity name (the item's name is mentioned in the input header, not the source text itself). The source text is a rule description, not a standalone entity.

entities.name: Weapon (javelin), uncommon

entities.name: KEOGHTOM's OINTMENT Wondrous item, uncommon

entities.name: LANTERN OF REVEALING Wondrous item, uncommon
